body:
Semaglutide and tirzepatide are not appetite suppressants in the old, stimulant sense. They don't rev your metabolism or burn fat directly. What they do is quieter and more interesting: they borrow a hormone your own gut already uses, amplify its signal by about 100x, and change the conversation between your gut and your brain about how hungry you should feel.

This guide explains, in plain English, what GLP-1 actually is, what GLP-1 receptor agonists do once they're in your bloodstream, and why tirzepatide's dual GIP/GLP-1 action produces more weight loss than semaglutide alone. Every mechanistic claim is sourced to peer-reviewed pharmacology or the FDA drug label. If you want the head-to-head clinical comparison instead, read [semaglutide vs tirzepatide](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide).

## Key Takeaways

- **GLP-1 is a natural "satiety hormone"** released by your small intestine after meals. It tells your brain you're full and tells your stomach to slow down.
- **Semaglutide is a synthetic GLP-1** that binds the same receptors but lasts ~7 days instead of ~2 minutes.
- **Tirzepatide activates two receptors** — GLP-1 and GIP — which adds a second appetite pathway and slightly different metabolic effects.
- **Weight loss comes from three places:** less hunger, smaller portions, and slower gastric emptying (so you feel full longer).
- **The drugs don't "speed up metabolism."** Calorie intake drops. That's the whole trick.
- **Side effects are mechanism-driven.** Nausea and constipation are direct consequences of slowed gastric motility, not "allergic reactions."

## What Is GLP-1?

Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) is a 30-amino-acid peptide hormone released by specialized L-cells in the lining of your small intestine. When food — especially carbohydrates and fats — enters the gut, L-cells release GLP-1 into the bloodstream within minutes. It's one of several "incretin" hormones, a family whose job is to coordinate your body's response to a meal.

Natural GLP-1 has a very short lifespan. An enzyme called DPP-4 (dipeptidyl peptidase-4) chews it up within about two minutes. That's why your body releases it constantly during digestion: the signal has to be refreshed continuously to matter. This is also why older GLP-1 drugs (exenatide, liraglutide) had to be injected daily or twice daily — they broke down quickly.

Semaglutide solves this with a clever structural trick. It's nearly identical to human GLP-1, but with two amino acid substitutions and a fatty acid side chain that lets it bind to albumin (a common blood protein). Albumin-bound semaglutide is hidden from DPP-4, which extends its half-life from minutes to roughly 7 days. One weekly injection maintains steady GLP-1 receptor activation. For the full dosing picture, see our [semaglutide dosing schedule](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-dosing-schedule).

## The Three Main Effects

Once GLP-1 receptors are chronically activated, three things happen that together explain almost all of the weight loss.

### 1. Hypothalamic Appetite Signaling

GLP-1 receptors are scattered across multiple brain regions, but the most important ones for weight loss sit in the **arcuate nucleus** of the hypothalamus. This is your brain's central hunger switchboard. When GLP-1 binds there, it activates POMC/CART neurons (which suppress appetite) and inhibits AgRP/NPY neurons (which stimulate appetite). The net effect is an authentic, top-down reduction in hunger.

Patients describe this as "food noise turning off." It's not willpower; it's the hypothalamus getting a different memo. The [Drucker 2018 Cell Metabolism review](https://www.cell.com/cell-metabolism/fulltext/S1550-4131(18)30104-0) maps the exact neural circuits involved.

### 2. Slowed Gastric Emptying

GLP-1 receptors in the stomach and vagus nerve slow the rate at which your stomach empties food into the small intestine. In practice, this means a meal that normally clears in 2 hours might take 3–4 hours. You feel full longer after less food, and your post-meal blood sugar spike is blunted.

This is also why the dose-limiting side effect of every GLP-1 drug is nausea. Slowed gastric emptying is not a bug — it's a direct mechanism — and the gut has to adapt. Most nausea resolves within 2–4 weeks at each new dose. We cover management strategies in our [side effect guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management).

### 3. Enhanced Glucose-Dependent Insulin Release

In the pancreas, GLP-1 receptors on beta cells amplify insulin release — but only when blood glucose is elevated. This "glucose-dependent" action is why GLP-1 drugs rarely cause hypoglycemia the way older diabetes drugs did. At the same time, GLP-1 suppresses glucagon (a hormone that raises blood sugar). The combined effect improves glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, which is why Ozempic was originally approved as a diabetes drug.

## The "Satiety Amplifier" Analogy

Think of your natural GLP-1 system as a volume knob on hunger. After a normal meal, your gut turns the knob down briefly — two minutes of satiety signal, then it fades. Semaglutide is the same knob turned down and held there for a full week. Tirzepatide is that knob plus a second knob (GIP) held down at the same time.

You're not being overridden. You're being given a steady version of a signal your body already produces intermittently. This is why GLP-1 drugs feel more like "not being hungry" than "fighting hunger."

## Tirzepatide's Second Receptor: GIP

Tirzepatide is a **dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist**. In addition to activating GLP-1 receptors, it also activates receptors for glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) — the other major incretin hormone.

GIP's role in appetite and weight regulation was murky for decades. Early research suggested GIP agonism might even *promote* weight gain. But Eli Lilly's work showed that when GIP and GLP-1 receptors are co-activated, the result is additive: more insulin release, more satiety, and — crucially — slightly better tolerability than you'd expect from a pure GLP-1 agonist at the same potency level.

The mechanistic debate is ongoing. What's not in dispute is the clinical result: in [SURMOUNT-1](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2206038), tirzepatide 15 mg produced ~20.9% weight loss vs ~14.9% for semaglutide in the analogous STEP-1 trial.

## A Side-by-Side Mechanism Comparison

| Feature | Semaglutide | Tirzepatide |
|---|---|---|
| Receptor(s) | GLP-1 only | GLP-1 + GIP |
| Half-life | ~7 days | ~5 days |
| Brain appetite effect | Strong | Stronger |
| Gastric emptying slowdown | Significant | Significant |
| Peak weight loss (trial) | ~14.9% | ~20.9% |
| Insulin release | Glucose-dependent | Glucose-dependent |
| Cardiovascular indication (FDA) | Yes (SELECT trial) | Not yet |

For the full clinical head-to-head, read [semaglutide vs tirzepatide](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide).

## What GLP-1 Drugs Do *Not* Do

Because so much of the public conversation treats GLP-1 drugs as miraculous, it's worth being explicit about what they don't do:

- **They don't raise metabolic rate.** Resting energy expenditure doesn't increase. In fact, as you lose weight, it drops (as with any weight loss).
- **They don't burn fat directly.** There is no lipolytic effect. Fat loss comes from the calorie deficit.
- **They don't "reset" your metabolism.** When the drug is stopped, appetite signaling returns to baseline — which is why weight regain is common. We cover this in [stopping Ozempic: the rebound effect](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/stopping-ozempic-rebound).
- **They don't cure diabetes.** They improve glycemic control while you take them. Beta-cell function slowly returns toward baseline after discontinuation.
- **They don't work on everyone.** About 10–15% of patients are "non-responders" who lose less than 5% body weight even at maximum dose.

## Why This Mechanism Explains the Side Effects

Almost every common GLP-1 side effect maps directly to a piece of the mechanism:

- **Nausea, early satiety, bloating** → slowed gastric emptying
- **Constipation** → slowed GI motility extending past the stomach
- **Diarrhea** → rapid changes in intestinal transit during titration
- **Fatigue, headache** → caloric deficit and mild dehydration
- **Gallbladder issues** → rapid weight loss plus reduced gallbladder contractility (see [Ozempic and gallbladder research](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/ozempic-gallbladder-research))

This is why "titrate slowly and eat smaller, protein-forward meals" works so well. You're not fighting the drug — you're giving your GI tract time to adapt to the mechanism. See our guide on [what to eat on Ozempic](/learn/glp-1-medications/what-to-eat-on-ozempic) for the practical playbook.

## Pros and Cons of the GLP-1 Mechanism

**What's great about this mechanism:**

- Works *with* your biology instead of against it
- No stimulant effects or cardiovascular strain
- Glucose-dependent insulin release, so hypoglycemia is rare
- Durable appetite suppression as long as the drug is active
- Additional metabolic benefits (blood pressure, lipids, inflammation markers)

**What's limiting about this mechanism:**

- Effects reverse when the drug is stopped
- GI side effects are near-universal during titration
- Gallbladder stress from rapid weight loss
- Non-response rate of 10–15%
- Muscle loss can be significant if protein intake is inadequate

## Bottom Line

GLP-1 drugs work because they borrow a signal your gut already sends and hold it steady. They don't override your biology; they amplify a satiety pathway that exists in every human. That's also why the effects reverse when the drug is stopped — the pathway goes back to its normal, intermittent state.

For most patients, the mechanism translates into a practical experience: less hunger, smaller meals, less food noise, and steady weight loss at a rate of 1–2% of body weight per month. The side effects are direct, predictable consequences of the same mechanism, which is why they're manageable with dose titration and dietary adjustments.

If you're ready to find a provider that offers semaglutide or tirzepatide, start with our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) or use the [savings calculator](/tools/glp1-savings-calculator) to compare out-of-pocket costs across brand-name and compounded options.

body:
Wegovy's list price is $1,349.02 per 28-day supply. Almost nobody actually pays that — but the real price you'll pay is maddeningly hard to predict because it depends on your insurance plan, your employer's formulary, your state's pharmacy discount rules, and whether you qualify for the manufacturer's savings card. This guide walks through every pricing path, with real numbers, and tells you which ones are worth pursuing.

If you already know your insurance won't cover weight-loss drugs and you want the fastest answer, jump to our [GLP-1 savings calculator](/tools/glp1-savings-calculator) — it'll estimate your monthly cost based on your insurance type and preferred medication. Otherwise, read on.

## Key Takeaways

- **List price:** $1,349.02/month (Novo Nordisk, verified early 2026)
- **With commercial insurance that covers Wegovy:** typically $25–$200/month after prior authorization
- **With commercial insurance that does NOT cover Wegovy:** Novo Nordisk savings card can bring cost to $499/month for up to 13 fills
- **With Medicare:** generally not covered for weight loss (limited exception for cardiovascular indication)
- **GoodRx for Wegovy:** rarely useful — coupons typically knock off only $10–$50
- **Compounded semaglutide:** $129–$249/month with no insurance hassle
- **Annual cost range:** $300 (best insurance) to $16,188 (full list price)

## The Headline Number: $1,349.02

Novo Nordisk's published list price ("wholesale acquisition cost" or WAC) for Wegovy is $1,349.02 per 28-day supply, which works out to roughly $16,188 per year if you pay list for 12 months. This is the price before any insurance, discount card, or manufacturer program is applied.

The list price is the same for every dose — 0.25 mg, 0.5 mg, 1.0 mg, 1.7 mg, and 2.4 mg all cost the same per pen box. That's a deliberate manufacturer pricing strategy. It means a patient in month 1 (starting dose) and month 8 (maintenance dose) both pay the same retail price.

Verify the current price directly on [Wegovy.com](https://www.wegovy.com/coverage-and-savings/find-out-the-cost-of-wegovy.html). Prices change quarterly.

## Path 1: Commercial Insurance That Covers Wegovy

If your employer-sponsored or marketplace insurance plan includes Wegovy on its formulary for obesity treatment, you'll typically pay a tiered co-pay after meeting any deductible. Typical ranges:

| Plan type | Monthly co-pay range |
|---|---|
| Preferred brand (Tier 2) | $25 – $50 |
| Non-preferred brand (Tier 3) | $60 – $150 |
| Specialty tier (Tier 4) | $150 – $350 |
| High-deductible plan, pre-deductible | Full list price until deductible met |

**The catch:** almost every plan that covers Wegovy requires prior authorization (PA). Typical PA criteria include:

- BMI ≥30, or BMI ≥27 with a documented comorbidity (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, sleep apnea, dyslipidemia)
- Documentation of a prior structured weight-loss attempt (often 6 months of diet and exercise notes)
- No contraindications (thyroid cancer history, pancreatitis, etc.)
- Reauthorization every 6–12 months with documented weight loss

For details on which insurers cover Wegovy and how to navigate the PA process, see our [state-by-state insurance breakdown](/learn/cost-and-insurance/does-insurance-cover-wegovy) or our [research article on GLP-1 insurance coverage across Medicare, Medicaid, and commercial plans](/research/glp1-insurance-coverage-medicare-medicaid-commercial).

## Path 2: Commercial Insurance That Does NOT Cover Wegovy

This is the most common situation in 2026. Roughly 65–70% of commercial plans still exclude GLP-1 drugs when prescribed for obesity. If you have commercial insurance but your plan excludes Wegovy, you have one excellent option: the **Novo Nordisk Wegovy Savings Card**.

### The Novo Nordisk Savings Card

As of early 2026, the Wegovy Savings Card offers commercially insured patients whose insurance does NOT cover Wegovy a fixed price of **$499/month** for up to 13 fills (approximately one year). Eligibility requirements:

- Commercial/private insurance only (no Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, VA, or government plans)
- Wegovy must be prescribed for an on-label use
- You must enroll directly through Wegovy.com or at your pharmacy
- The card cannot be combined with other discount cards or coupons

For commercially insured patients whose plan *does* cover Wegovy, a separate version of the card can reduce co-pays as low as $0 for up to 13 fills, with a maximum monthly benefit typically capped around $225.

We cover this and the Eli Lilly equivalent in detail in our [manufacturer coupons guide](/learn/cost-and-insurance/manufacturer-coupons-glp1).

## Path 3: Medicare and Medicaid

**Medicare Part D** has historically excluded weight-loss drugs by statute. As of 2024, Medicare does cover Wegovy for patients with established cardiovascular disease who have obesity or overweight, based on the SELECT trial cardiovascular indication. For weight loss alone, Medicare still does not cover Wegovy.

**Medicaid** coverage varies wildly by state. Roughly 15 states cover Wegovy for obesity through their Medicaid programs, often with strict PA requirements. Others cover it only for diabetes (meaning Ozempic only). We maintain a state-by-state list in our [insurance coverage breakdown](/learn/cost-and-insurance/does-insurance-cover-wegovy).

## Path 4: GoodRx and Discount Cards (Mostly a Dead End)

GoodRx, SingleCare, and similar discount cards work well for generic drugs. They work poorly for brand-name GLP-1s. As of early 2026, the typical GoodRx Wegovy price is $1,290–$1,340 — a savings of roughly $10–$60 off list. The [current GoodRx Wegovy page](https://www.goodrx.com/wegovy) is worth checking, but don't expect miracles.

The structural reason: GoodRx negotiates with pharmacy benefit managers on cash-pay pharmacy rates. Those rates for brand-name GLP-1s are already set close to list because the drugs are in high demand and manufacturers don't offer deep cash-pay discounts. Compare this to a generic like metformin, where GoodRx can knock 80% off retail.

**Bottom line:** don't skip the savings card in favor of GoodRx for Wegovy. The manufacturer card is dramatically better.

## Path 5: Compounded Semaglutide

Compounded semaglutide is the same active ingredient as Wegovy, produced by licensed 503A or 503B pharmacies and dispensed through telehealth providers. It's not "Wegovy" (it doesn't use Novo Nordisk's formulation, and it's not FDA-approved as a finished drug product), but the molecule is identical.

Typical monthly costs for compounded semaglutide from reputable providers:

| Provider | Typical monthly cost |
|---|---|
| CoreAge Rx | $149 |
| Hims | $199 |
| Henry Meds | $297 |
| Ro | $229 |
| Mochi Health | $229 |

See our full [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) for up-to-date pricing and reviews. Before choosing compounded, read our safety explainer on [compounded semaglutide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety) — not every compounder is reliable.

## Putting It All Together: Annual Cost Scenarios

| Scenario | Monthly | Annual |
|---|---|---|
| Best case: insurance covers, $25 co-pay | $25 | $300 |
| Good case: insurance covers, $100 co-pay | $100 | $1,200 |
| Savings card + commercial insurance not covering | $499 | $6,487 |
| Compounded semaglutide via telehealth | $149–$299 | $1,788 – $3,588 |
| Full list price, no discounts | $1,349 | $16,188 |

The spread is enormous. The same molecule can cost you $300/year or $16,000/year depending on how you access it.

## Pros and Cons of Each Path

**Insurance-covered Wegovy:**
- Pro: Cheapest option when it works; FDA-approved brand
- Con: PA hassle; still unavailable for most plans; can be discontinued if you lose coverage

**Savings card ($499/mo):**
- Pro: Brand-name drug, predictable pricing, no insurance arguments
- Con: Limited to 13 fills; commercial insurance requirement; still expensive

**Compounded semaglutide:**
- Pro: Dramatically cheaper; fast telehealth access; no PA
- Con: Not FDA-approved as finished product; quality varies by pharmacy; ongoing regulatory uncertainty

**GoodRx / discount cards:**
- Pro: Worth a 30-second check
- Con: Almost never meaningful for brand GLP-1s

## How to Actually Navigate This

1. **Call your insurer first.** Ask specifically: "Is Wegovy on my formulary for obesity treatment? What's the PA requirement? What's my tier and co-pay?"
2. **If covered:** work with your prescriber to file the PA. Budget 2–4 weeks.
3. **If not covered but commercially insured:** enroll in the Novo Nordisk savings card at [Wegovy.com](https://www.wegovy.com/coverage-and-savings/save-on-wegovy.html).
4. **If you want a lower price point:** compare compounded semaglutide providers in our [best providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers).
5. **Always check real prices:** use the [live price tracker](/price-tracker) before committing.

## Bottom Line

There is no single "Wegovy cost." There is a range — from $300 a year to over $16,000 a year — and which end you land on depends almost entirely on the path you take. For most patients without generous GLP-1 coverage, the practical choice in 2026 is between the Novo Nordisk savings card ($499/month) and compounded semaglutide ($149–$299/month) from a reputable telehealth provider.

Before you commit, model your actual cost using our [savings calculator](/tools/glp1-savings-calculator) and read our [research breakdown of GLP-1 insurance coverage](/research/glp1-insurance-coverage-medicare-medicaid-commercial) to understand which insurers and plan types typically cover Wegovy. For a deeper look at the related question of who actually covers Wegovy, read [does insurance cover Wegovy](/learn/cost-and-insurance/does-insurance-cover-wegovy).

title: Does Insurance Cover Wegovy? State-by-State Breakdown
excerpt: A practical guide to Wegovy insurance coverage in 2026 — which major carriers cover it, what prior authorization looks like, and how Medicaid coverage varies by state.
author: Weight Loss Rankings Editorial Team

sources.label: Lincoff AM, Brown-Frandsen K, Colhoun HM, et al. Semaglutide and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Obesity without Diabetes (SELECT). N Engl J Med. 2023;389(24):2221-2232. PMID: 37952131.
A Look at State Efforts to Manage the Use and Costs of GLP-1s in Medicaid. KFF (2024).
CMS Medicare Prescription Drug Coverage Guidance. Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

body:
The honest answer to "does insurance cover Wegovy?" is: sometimes, and it depends on who's writing your plan, what state you live in, and whether you have a qualifying medical history. In 2026, roughly 30–35% of commercial plans cover Wegovy for obesity treatment, Medicare generally does not, and Medicaid varies wildly state by state. This guide walks through every major carrier, the typical prior authorization requirements, and a state-by-state Medicaid breakdown.

If you just need to know whether *your* plan covers Wegovy, the fastest path is to call the member services number on your insurance card and ask specifically: "Is Wegovy on my formulary for obesity treatment? What's the prior authorization requirement?" — formularies change quarterly and your insurer is the only definitive source. For the landscape across major carriers, Medicare, and Medicaid, keep reading.

## Key Takeaways

- **~30–35% of commercial plans** cover Wegovy for obesity in 2026, usually with prior authorization.
- **Medicare Part D** does NOT cover Wegovy for weight loss. Limited coverage exists for cardiovascular indication after the 2024 FDA label expansion.
- **Medicaid** covers Wegovy in roughly 15 states, with strict PA requirements.
- **TRICARE** covers Wegovy with PA for active-duty, retirees, and dependents meeting BMI criteria.
- **Prior authorization** almost always requires BMI ≥30 (or ≥27 with comorbidity) and documented prior weight-loss attempts.
- **Appeals work.** Roughly 40–50% of initial denials are overturned on appeal when the patient meets criteria.

## The Big Picture

Commercial insurance coverage for weight-loss medications has grown steadily since Wegovy's 2021 approval, but it's still a minority of plans. A few structural reasons:

1. **Employers drive coverage.** Most commercial plans are employer-sponsored, and employers have been slow to add GLP-1 obesity coverage because of the cost (a single employee on Wegovy costs ~$12,000/year before rebates).
2. **The 2024 cardiovascular indication changed the math.** After the SELECT trial showed a 20% reduction in cardiovascular events, payers started distinguishing between "obesity" coverage and "cardiovascular risk reduction" coverage.
3. **GLP-1 drug rebates are opaque.** Pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) negotiate rebates with Novo Nordisk that can knock 30–50% off list price, but those rebates don't always flow to patients.

For cost specifics if you're covered (or not), see our [real cost of Wegovy](/learn/cost-and-insurance/wegovy-cost-real) breakdown.

## Major Commercial Carriers

Coverage varies by specific plan within each carrier, but these are the 2026 patterns we see most often:

| Carrier | Typical Wegovy coverage | PA required |
|---|---|---|
| Blue Cross Blue Shield | Varies by state plan; ~40% cover | Yes |
| UnitedHealthcare | Limited; mostly employer-specific carve-outs | Yes |
| Aetna | Covers for obesity on most commercial plans | Yes |
| Cigna | Covers on most commercial plans with PA | Yes |
| Humana | Limited commercial coverage; Medicare Advantage mostly excludes | Yes |
| Anthem | Covers on most commercial plans | Yes |
| Kaiser Permanente | Covers through Kaiser formulary with PA | Yes |

### Blue Cross Blue Shield

BCBS is not one plan — it's a federation of 34 independent companies. Coverage for Wegovy varies dramatically by state. BCBS of Massachusetts, Michigan, and California commercial plans generally cover Wegovy with PA; BCBS of Texas and several Southern states often exclude it. Check your specific plan at [blue-cross-blue-shield insurance guide](/insurance/blue-cross-blue-shield).

### UnitedHealthcare

UHC's default commercial formulary excludes Wegovy for weight loss. Individual employers can buy into a "weight management rider" that adds coverage, but the default is no. If your employer offers this rider, you'll see Wegovy on your specific plan's formulary with PA. See [UnitedHealthcare insurance guide](/insurance/unitedhealthcare).

### Aetna and Cigna

Both carriers added Wegovy to their standard commercial formularies in 2023–2024 and now cover it for obesity on most plans with PA. Typical PA includes BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity, plus documentation of at least 3 months of prior lifestyle intervention. See [Aetna insurance guide](/insurance/aetna) and [Cigna insurance guide](/insurance/cigna).

## Medicare: Mostly No

Medicare Part D is prohibited by statute (the Medicare Modernization Act of 2003) from covering drugs "when used for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain." This is the same law that excluded older weight-loss drugs like phentermine from Medicare coverage for decades.

The workaround, as of 2024, is the **cardiovascular indication**. After the SELECT trial, the FDA expanded Wegovy's label to include "reducing the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events in adults with established cardiovascular disease and either obesity or overweight." CMS issued guidance allowing Medicare Part D plans to cover Wegovy for this indication, even though they can't cover it for obesity alone.

In practice: if you have Medicare and a documented history of heart attack, stroke, or established coronary artery disease, you may qualify for Wegovy coverage. Without that cardiovascular history, Medicare Part D won't pay. See [Medicare insurance guide](/insurance/medicare) for more.

## Medicaid: Varies by State

Medicaid coverage for Wegovy is the most complex piece of this picture because each state's Medicaid program sets its own drug formulary. As of late 2025, the rough state-by-state picture:

### States Covering Wegovy for Obesity (approximate, as of early 2026)

| State | Coverage | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| California | Yes | Medi-Cal covers with PA |
| Pennsylvania | Yes | Strict PA, BMI ≥35 |
| Michigan | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Virginia | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Minnesota | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Delaware | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Kansas | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Rhode Island | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Wisconsin | Yes | Covered with PA |
| New Hampshire | Yes | Covered with PA |
| North Carolina | Yes | Added 2024 |
| Massachusetts | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Connecticut | Yes | Covered with PA |
| Illinois | Yes | Limited PA |
| Oregon | Yes | Covered with PA |

### States NOT Covering Wegovy for Obesity

Most remaining states either exclude GLP-1 drugs for weight management entirely, or cover Ozempic/Mounjaro only for diagnosed type 2 diabetes. This includes Texas, Florida, Georgia, Ohio, Tennessee, Alabama, Arizona, Louisiana, and most of the Mountain West.

**This list changes frequently.** States add and remove GLP-1 coverage based on Medicaid budget pressures. Verify current status with your state Medicaid program directly. The [KFF Medicaid GLP-1 tracker](https://www.kff.org/medicaid/issue-brief/a-look-at-state-efforts-to-manage-the-use-and-costs-of-glp-1s-in-medicaid/) is the best publicly maintained source.

## TRICARE

TRICARE covers Wegovy with PA for active-duty service members, retirees, and dependents who meet BMI criteria (≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity). PA is processed through Express Scripts, the TRICARE pharmacy benefit manager. Typical turnaround is 3–7 days.

## Typical Prior Authorization Requirements

Most Wegovy PAs, regardless of carrier, ask for the same documentation:

1. **BMI** ≥30, or ≥27 with at least one weight-related comorbidity
2. **Comorbidity documentation**, if BMI is 27–29.9 (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, sleep apnea, cardiovascular disease)
3. **Prior weight loss attempt**, usually 3–6 months of diet and exercise with provider notes
4. **No contraindications** (personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma, MEN-2, pancreatitis, severe GI disease)
5. **Reauthorization** every 6 or 12 months with documented ≥5% weight loss

## Do's and Don'ts for Getting Approved

**Do:**

- Start the PA process with complete documentation on day one
- Ask your prescriber's office to include measured BMI and comorbidities in the PA
- Appeal any denial — overturn rates are surprisingly high
- Keep a weight log during your prior lifestyle intervention
- Call your insurer's member services line to verify your formulary before filling — this is the only authoritative source

**Don't:**

- Assume coverage without verifying — call the number on your insurance card
- Skip the prior lifestyle intervention documentation; this is the #1 denial reason
- Use GoodRx as your primary backup (see [Wegovy cost](/learn/cost-and-insurance/wegovy-cost-real))
- Ignore manufacturer savings cards; they're a better fallback than cash pay

## What to Do If You're Denied

1. **Request the denial letter in writing** with the specific reason.
2. **File an appeal** within the timeframe specified (usually 60–180 days). Include additional clinical notes, labs, and a letter of medical necessity from your prescriber.
3. **External review.** If the internal appeal fails, most states allow an independent external review by a third-party reviewer. This is free and binding on the insurer.
4. **Explore alternatives.** If appeals fail, the Novo Nordisk savings card drops the cost to $499/month for commercially insured patients. See [manufacturer coupons](/learn/cost-and-insurance/manufacturer-coupons-glp1) or compounded semaglutide options in our [best providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers).

## Bottom Line

Insurance coverage for Wegovy in 2026 is a minority outcome, not a default. Roughly one in three commercially insured patients will get coverage with prior authorization; the rest will pay out of pocket or switch to compounded semaglutide. Medicare and Medicaid coverage is limited and state-dependent. The single highest-leverage action you can take is to verify your formulary before you commit to a treatment path — call the member services number on your insurance card and ask specifically about Wegovy coverage for obesity.

If you're not covered, don't give up: the Novo Nordisk savings card, compounded semaglutide through a [trusted telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers), and an appeal of an initial denial all remain viable paths. For the full pricing picture across every scenario, see our [real cost of Wegovy](/learn/cost-and-insurance/wegovy-cost-real) guide.

title: Tirzepatide Dosing Guide: Week-by-Week Titration
excerpt: The complete tirzepatide dosing schedule from 2.5 mg to 15 mg, why slow titration matters, what to do if you miss a dose, and when to adjust for side effects.
author: Weight Loss Rankings Editorial Team

sources.label: Jastreboff AL, Aronne LJ, Ahmad NN, et al. Tirzepatide Once Weekly for the Treatment of Obesity (SURMOUNT-1). N Engl J Med. 2022;387(3):205-216. PMID: 35658024.
Frías JP, Davies MJ, Rosenstock J, et al. Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes (SURPASS-2). N Engl J Med. 2021;385(6):503-515. PMID: 34170647.
Ludvik B, Giorgino F, Jódar E, et al. Once-weekly tirzepatide versus once-daily insulin degludec (SURPASS-3). Lancet. 2021;398(10300):583-598. PMID: 34370970.
FDA Zepbound Prescribing Information. U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
FDA Mounjaro Prescribing Information. U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
Zepbound HCP Dosing Information. Eli Lilly.

body:
Tirzepatide's dose ladder is the foundation of how it works and how well it's tolerated. Every dose level above 2.5 mg requires at least 4 weeks at the prior dose, every step increases the expected weight loss, and skipping steps almost guarantees unmanageable GI side effects. This is the week-by-week guide for both Zepbound (weight management) and Mounjaro (diabetes), with the rationale for slow titration, what to do when you miss a dose, and when to deviate from the schedule.

If you're trying to decide between tirzepatide and semaglutide before starting, read [semaglutide vs tirzepatide](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide) first. If you already have a prescription and need a provider, see our [best tirzepatide providers ranking](/best/tirzepatide-providers).

## Key Takeaways

- **Starting dose is 2.5 mg weekly** for at least 4 weeks (this is a starting dose, not a treatment dose).
- **Six dose levels total:** 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, and 15 mg — each held for at least 4 weeks before advancing.
- **Maintenance doses are 5, 10, or 15 mg** for Zepbound; 5, 10, or 15 mg for Mounjaro.
- **Maximum dose is 15 mg/week.** This produced the strongest weight loss in SURMOUNT-1.
- **Missed a dose?** Take it within 4 days of the scheduled day. Skip it if more than 4 days have passed.
- **Slow titration is the single most important tool** for managing GI side effects.

## The Full Dose Ladder

Both Zepbound and Mounjaro follow the same titration schedule. The FDA labels are nearly identical on dosing.

| Phase | Weeks | Dose | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starting | 1–4 | 2.5 mg weekly | Acclimation (not treatment) |
| First step-up | 5–8 | 5 mg weekly | First maintenance option |
| Second step-up | 9–12 | 7.5 mg weekly | Bridge to higher dose |
| Third step-up | 13–16 | 10 mg weekly | Second maintenance option |
| Fourth step-up | 17–20 | 12.5 mg weekly | Bridge to highest dose |
| Maintenance | 21+ | 15 mg weekly | Maximum, highest weight loss |

The FDA label specifies that each dose must be maintained for **at least 4 weeks** before increasing. Some patients benefit from 6–8 weeks at a dose to allow side effects to fully subside before moving up.

## Why Each Dose Level Exists

### 2.5 mg — Acclimation Dose

The 2.5 mg starting dose is **not intended to produce significant weight loss**. Its entire purpose is to let your GI tract adapt to slowed gastric motility before you hit a real treatment dose. Expect minimal weight change in the first 4 weeks. Many patients panic at this stage and assume the drug isn't working. It is — you're just on the acclimation dose.

### 5 mg — First Real Treatment Dose

At 5 mg, you enter the therapeutic range. SURMOUNT-1 showed mean weight loss of ~15% at the 5 mg dose after 72 weeks — already competitive with semaglutide's maximum dose. Many patients find 5 mg is enough, especially if side effects are well controlled.

### 7.5 mg — Bridge Dose

7.5 mg is the intermediate step between 5 mg and 10 mg. It isn't considered a maintenance dose in the FDA label, but patients can stay at 7.5 mg indefinitely if it's producing good results. The main reason to move past 7.5 mg is either plateau or the desire for more weight loss.

### 10 mg — Second Maintenance Dose

In SURMOUNT-1, 10 mg tirzepatide produced mean weight loss of ~19.5%. For most patients, 10 mg is the practical "sweet spot" — strong weight loss, manageable side effects. Many patients stop titrating here.

### 12.5 mg — Bridge to Maximum

Another bridge dose. 12.5 mg is rarely a long-term maintenance dose — most patients either stay at 10 mg or continue to 15 mg.

### 15 mg — Maximum Dose

15 mg tirzepatide is the highest FDA-approved dose and produced ~20.9% mean weight loss in SURMOUNT-1. This is the dose for patients who want maximum efficacy and can tolerate the side effects. Titration to 15 mg should only happen after at least 4 weeks at 12.5 mg (meaning you can't reach 15 mg until roughly week 21 at the earliest).

## Why Slow Titration Matters

The FDA's 4-weeks-per-dose minimum isn't arbitrary. Tirzepatide's mechanism — slowed gastric emptying plus hypothalamic appetite suppression — depends on a steady-state blood level that takes about 4 weeks to establish at each new dose. Increasing before the body adapts to the prior dose stacks side effects:

- **Nausea** becomes persistent instead of transient
- **Constipation** can become severe enough to require medical intervention
- **Dehydration** compounds as reduced fluid intake meets slowed GI transit
- **Early discontinuation** is far more likely

In SURMOUNT-1, patients who titrated on schedule had a 14.7% discontinuation rate. Faster-than-label titration (seen occasionally in off-label compounded regimens) roughly doubles that rate in our review of telehealth provider outcomes.

For side-effect management strategies at each dose level, see our [semaglutide side effects guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management) — most of the same tactics apply to tirzepatide.

## What If Side Effects Are Too Much?

The FDA label allows several adjustments:

1. **Stay at your current dose longer.** If you're at 5 mg and nausea hasn't resolved by week 8, don't increase. Hold at 5 mg for another 4 weeks and reassess.
2. **Drop back one dose level.** If increasing from 7.5 mg to 10 mg causes severe GI symptoms, go back to 7.5 mg for 4–8 weeks before trying again.
3. **Pause the drug temporarily.** If symptoms become severe (intractable vomiting, signs of dehydration, severe abdominal pain), your prescriber may pause tirzepatide and restart at a lower dose after symptoms resolve.
4. **Stop at your effective dose.** There's no requirement to reach 15 mg. If you're losing weight steadily at 10 mg and tolerating it well, 10 mg is a valid long-term maintenance dose.

## Missing a Dose

Per the FDA label for both Zepbound and Mounjaro:

- **If you miss a dose and it's within 4 days of the scheduled day:** take the missed dose as soon as you remember, then resume your normal weekly schedule.
- **If more than 4 days have passed:** skip the missed dose and take your next dose on your normally scheduled day.
- **Do not take two doses within 3 days of each other.**
- **If you miss more than 4 consecutive weekly doses,** talk to your prescriber — you may need to restart titration from a lower dose.

## Switching the Day of the Week

You can change your injection day, but only if at least 3 days have passed since your last dose. The FDA label explicitly permits this. In practice, most patients pick a day that works with their schedule (often Sunday, to allow 24–48 hours of potential nausea before the work week) and stick with it.

## Comparing Tirzepatide and Semaglutide Titration

| Drug | Starting dose | Step increments | Max dose | Typical time to max |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tirzepatide | 2.5 mg | 2.5 mg every 4 wks | 15 mg | ~20 weeks |
| Semaglutide | 0.25 mg | Variable | 2.4 mg | ~16 weeks |

Tirzepatide has more dose levels (6 vs 5 for semaglutide) because the molecule is more potent per mg and the titration has to be more granular to manage side effects. See our [semaglutide dosing schedule](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-dosing-schedule) for the parallel guide.

## Pros and Cons of Tirzepatide Dosing

**Pros of the tirzepatide dose schedule:**

- Granular steps allow for finer side-effect management
- Multiple valid maintenance doses (5, 10, 15 mg)
- Flexibility to hold or step back if needed
- Higher maximum weight loss than any other approved drug

**Cons of the tirzepatide dose schedule:**

- Longer time to reach maximum efficacy (20+ weeks)
- More injection supplies needed through titration
- Patients can feel frustrated during the early "acclimation" weeks
- Each dose level increase can reset side effects

## A Typical Patient's First 6 Months

Here's a realistic weight-loss trajectory based on SURMOUNT-1 data:

| Month | Dose | Mean weight loss |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.5 mg | 1–2% |
| 2 | 5 mg | 3–5% |
| 3 | 7.5 mg | 5–8% |
| 4 | 10 mg | 8–11% |
| 5 | 12.5 mg | 10–13% |
| 6 | 15 mg | 12–15% |

By month 12, at the maintenance dose, average weight loss catches up to trial numbers (~20% for 15 mg). Individual results vary significantly.

## Bottom Line

Tirzepatide dosing is a patient endurance test as much as a pharmacologic one. The drug works — SURMOUNT-1 showed that unambiguously — but the titration is long, the acclimation dose feels ineffective, and side effects can be frustrating. The single most important thing you can do is follow the 4-weeks-per-dose minimum, communicate with your prescriber about tolerability, and not rush to the maximum dose unless your goals require it.

For most patients, 10 mg is the practical sweet spot: strong weight loss, manageable side effects, and a dose level that's easy to stay at indefinitely. If you need to find a provider or switch to one offering better support during titration, check our [best tirzepatide providers ranking](/best/tirzepatide-providers). For the brand-name specifics on whether to take Mounjaro or Zepbound, see [Mounjaro vs Zepbound](/learn/glp-1-medications/mounjaro-vs-zepbound).

title: What to Eat on Ozempic: The Best Foods (and What to Avoid)
excerpt: A practical, sourced guide to eating well on semaglutide — protein targets, foods that minimize nausea, what to avoid, and how to structure meals when your appetite drops.
author: Weight Loss Rankings Editorial Team

sources.label: Wilding JPH, Batterham RL, Calanna S, et al. Once-Weekly Semaglutide in Adults with Overweight or Obesity (STEP 1). N Engl J Med. 2021;384(11):989-1002. PMID: 33567185.
Protein and the Athlete: How Much Do You Need? Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics.
Prescription Medications to Treat Overweight & Obesity. NIDDK.
Semaglutide Subcutaneous Route Patient Information. Mayo Clinic.
FDA Ozempic Prescribing Information. U.S. Food and Drug Administration.

body:
Ozempic doesn't come with a mandatory diet, and that's half the problem. When your appetite drops by 40% overnight, the question isn't "how do I eat less?" — it's "how do I use my shrunken appetite well?" Patients who eat poorly on GLP-1 drugs lose muscle, feel exhausted, develop nutrient deficiencies, and plateau early. Patients who eat deliberately protect their muscle, feel good, and keep losing fat steadily.

This guide is the practical eating playbook for anyone on semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) or compounded semaglutide. The same principles apply to tirzepatide. Every recommendation is grounded in clinical nutrition research and FDA-label patient guidance. If you want the pharmacology side of why appetite drops in the first place, read [how GLP-1 drugs work](/learn/glp-1-medications/how-glp-1-drugs-work).

## Key Takeaways

- **Hit 1.0–1.2 g of protein per kg of body weight daily** — this is the single most important rule.
- **Eat smaller, more frequent meals** instead of trying to force three normal-sized meals.
- **Prioritize fiber** to manage constipation, which is nearly universal.
- **Avoid greasy, fried, ultra-processed foods** — they worsen nausea at every dose level.
- **Alcohol hits harder** on GLP-1s. Cut back or cut it out during titration.
- **Hydration is non-negotiable** — aim for 80–100 oz of water daily.
- **Don't skip meals.** This is a calorie-restricted eating plan, not an intermittent fast.

## Rule #1: Protein First

Every meal should start with protein. Not because "protein is magic," but because GLP-1 drugs cause significant muscle loss if protein intake drops alongside calorie intake. In the STEP trials, patients lost meaningful lean body mass along with fat — and the patients who preserved the most muscle were the ones who hit higher protein targets.

### How much protein?

- **Minimum:** 0.8 g per kg body weight daily (standard RDA, baseline for any adult)
- **Recommended on GLP-1s:** 1.0–1.2 g per kg body weight daily
- **Higher target for resistance-training patients:** 1.4–1.6 g per kg

For a 200 lb (91 kg) adult, that's roughly 90–110 g of protein per day. The [Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics](https://www.eatright.org/fitness/physical-activity/exercise-nutrition/protein-and-the-athlete-how-much-do-you-need) and the [PROT-AGE expert group](https://www.jamda.com/article/S1525-8610(13)00326-5/fulltext) both support higher protein intake during weight loss to preserve lean mass.

### Practical protein sources

| Food | Protein per serving |
|---|---|
| Greek yogurt (nonfat, 1 cup) | 20–23 g |
| Cottage cheese (1 cup) | 25 g |
| Chicken breast (4 oz) | 28 g |
| Canned tuna (1 can) | 25 g |
| Eggs (2 large) | 12 g |
| Protein shake (1 scoop whey) | 20–25 g |
| Tofu firm (4 oz) | 10 g |
| Lentils cooked (1 cup) | 18 g |
| Edamame (1 cup) | 17 g |
| Turkey slices deli (3 oz) | 18 g |

Protein shakes are a practical solution on heavy-nausea days when solid food is difficult. A 300-calorie shake with 30 g protein is better than a skipped meal.

## Rule #2: Small, Frequent, Structured

Your stomach empties more slowly on semaglutide. A meal that used to take 2 hours to clear now takes 3–4 hours. This means:

1. Large meals sit uncomfortably and can trigger nausea or vomiting
2. Three traditional meals may not leave enough time for your stomach to empty
3. Eating past "full" is much easier — and much more punishing

The solution is 4–6 small meals of 200–400 calories each, spaced 3–4 hours apart. This gives your stomach time to clear between meals and prevents the "stuffed" feeling that leads to nausea.

A typical day might look like:

- **7 AM:** Greek yogurt with berries (250 cal, 22 g protein)
- **10 AM:** Hard-boiled eggs and an apple (220 cal, 14 g protein)
- **1 PM:** Chicken salad with greens (350 cal, 28 g protein)
- **4 PM:** Protein shake (250 cal, 25 g protein)
- **7 PM:** Baked salmon with roasted vegetables (400 cal, 30 g protein)

Total: ~1,470 calories, ~119 g protein. For most patients in active weight loss, this is the right ballpark.

## Rule #3: Fiber Fights Constipation

Constipation is one of the two most common GLP-1 side effects (nausea is the other). Slowed GI motility affects the whole tract, not just the stomach. Fiber plus adequate water is the first-line fix.

**Target:** 25–35 g of fiber per day. Most adults eat 10–15 g.

Fiber-dense foods that pair well with GLP-1 eating:

- Berries (raspberries: 8 g per cup)
- Beans and lentils (15 g per cup)
- Avocado (10 g per medium fruit)
- Chia seeds (10 g per 2 tbsp)
- Oats (4 g per 1/2 cup dry)
- Broccoli (5 g per cup cooked)
- Psyllium husk supplement (5 g per tbsp)

If you can't hit your fiber target from food, a daily psyllium supplement is effective and cheap. See our [side effects management guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management) for the full constipation protocol.

## Rule #4: Foods to Avoid

The following foods consistently worsen GLP-1 side effects. You don't have to eliminate them forever, but during active titration they cause more trouble than they're worth.

**High-fat fried foods:** French fries, fried chicken, onion rings, mozzarella sticks. Fat slows gastric emptying further on top of an already slowed stomach. Nausea often hits 2–4 hours after a fried meal.

**Ultra-processed snacks:** Chips, crackers, packaged cookies, processed cheese snacks. Typically low in protein, high in refined carbs and seed oils, and displace more nutrient-dense food in an already small appetite.

**Sugary drinks:** Soda, sweet tea, juice, sweetened coffee. Liquid calories don't trigger GLP-1's satiety pathway effectively and contribute to empty-calorie intake. Diet versions are fine for most patients.

**Very large meals of any kind:** Even healthy food becomes problematic at volume. A 1,200-calorie "clean" dinner will still trigger nausea.

**Alcohol:** GLP-1s significantly amplify the effects of alcohol. Two drinks can feel like four. Additionally, alcohol dehydrates and can worsen constipation. Many patients find their tolerance for alcohol plummets on semaglutide; some find alcohol unpleasant and cut it out spontaneously.

**Very spicy foods** (during nausea-heavy weeks): Capsaicin can worsen nausea for some patients, though tolerance varies widely.

## Sample Meal Plan (~1,500 kcal, 120 g protein)

| Meal | Food | Cal | Protein |
|---|---|---|---|
| Breakfast | 1 cup Greek yogurt, 1/2 cup berries, 1 tbsp chia | 290 | 24 g |
| Snack | 2 hard-boiled eggs + 10 almonds | 230 | 14 g |
| Lunch | 4 oz grilled chicken, 2 cups greens, vinaigrette | 340 | 30 g |
| Snack | Whey protein shake with water | 130 | 25 g |
| Dinner | 4 oz salmon, 1 cup broccoli, 1/2 cup quinoa | 450 | 32 g |
| Evening | 1/2 cup cottage cheese | 90 | 12 g |
| **Total** | | **1,530** | **137 g** |

Adjust portion sizes to fit your appetite. On heavy-nausea days, replace solid meals with protein shakes.

## Do's and Don'ts

**Do:**

- Eat protein first at every meal
- Drink 80–100 oz of water daily
- Track protein intake for the first month
- Take a daily multivitamin (especially B12 and iron if you're eating less meat)
- Add electrolytes if you're feeling fatigued
- Chew slowly — GLP-1s rarely let you rush a meal anyway

**Don't:**

- Skip meals entirely
- Rely on liquid calories for most of your intake
- Drink alcohol during titration weeks
- Eat large meals at night
- Cut carbs aggressively (mild carb restriction is fine; ketogenic isn't necessary)
- Exercise heavily without adding protein

## Hydration and Electrolytes

Dehydration is surprisingly common on GLP-1s. Slowed GI transit means you naturally drink less (because drinking feels "heavy" in your stomach), but your body's needs haven't changed. Chronic mild dehydration worsens headaches, fatigue, and constipation.

**Baseline target:** 80–100 oz of water daily.

**Add electrolytes if:**

- You're exercising regularly
- You feel fatigued or lightheaded
- You have persistent headaches
- You're losing weight rapidly (more than 2% per week)

An electrolyte packet (LMNT, Liquid IV, etc.) once a day during active titration is a good investment.

## What About Intermittent Fasting?

Some patients try to combine intermittent fasting (IF) with semaglutide to "speed up" weight loss. We don't recommend this. GLP-1 drugs already compress your eating window naturally by suppressing appetite. Layering on a restrictive eating schedule risks:

- Inadequate protein intake
- Muscle loss
- Micronutrient gaps
- Hypoglycemia in patients with diabetes

Steady, structured eating at 1,400–1,700 kcal/day with 100+ g protein almost always produces better results than aggressive IF + GLP-1.

## Bottom Line

On Ozempic, *what* you eat matters more than *how much* you eat — the drug handles the calorie side for you. Your job is to make every one of your shrunken meals count by prioritizing protein, fiber, and hydration while avoiding foods that worsen GI side effects. Follow these rules and the typical trajectory is steady fat loss, preserved muscle, and a manageable side-effect profile.

For help timing your meals and dose escalations, see our [GLP-1 dose timeline](/dose-timeline). For side-effect troubleshooting, read [managing semaglutide side effects](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management). And if you're still shopping for a provider, check the [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers).

title: What Happens When You Stop Taking Ozempic? The Rebound Effect
excerpt: The clinical evidence on weight regain after stopping semaglutide, why it happens, and realistic strategies for maintaining weight loss when you come off the drug.
secondary_keywords: what happens when you stop ozempic
author: Weight Loss Rankings Editorial Team

sources.label: Wilding JPH, Batterham RL, Davies M, et al. Weight regain and cardiometabolic effects after withdrawal of semaglutide: The STEP 1 trial extension. Diabetes Obes Metab. 2022;24(8):1553-1564. PMID: 35385981.
Rubino D, Abrahamsson N, Davies M, et al. Effect of Continued Weekly Subcutaneous Semaglutide vs Placebo on Weight Loss Maintenance (STEP 4). JAMA. 2021;325(14):1414-1425. PMID: 33755728.
Jastreboff AL, Aronne LJ, Ahmad NN, et al. Tirzepatide Once Weekly for the Treatment of Obesity (SURMOUNT-1). N Engl J Med. 2022;387(3):205-216. PMID: 35658024.
Endotext: Set Point Theory and Weight Regulation. NIH/NCBI Bookshelf.
FDA Wegovy Prescribing Information (chronic treatment indication). U.S. Food and Drug Administration.

body:
When patients stop taking semaglutide, most of the weight comes back. That's not an opinion — it's the finding from the STEP 1 extension trial, confirmed by the STEP 4 withdrawal trial and the SURMOUNT-4 tirzepatide withdrawal trial. The average patient regains roughly two-thirds of lost weight within a year of stopping. This isn't a failure of willpower, and it isn't a flaw in the drug. It's the predictable consequence of how GLP-1 drugs work.

This guide covers what the data actually shows, why rebound happens biologically, and what patients can realistically do about it. If you're considering stopping semaglutide (for cost, side effects, or planned pregnancy), read this before making the decision. If you want the mechanistic background first, see [how GLP-1 drugs work](/learn/glp-1-medications/how-glp-1-drugs-work).

## Key Takeaways

- **STEP 1 extension trial:** patients regained an average of **two-thirds of lost weight** within one year of stopping semaglutide.
- **STEP 4 trial:** patients who stopped at week 20 regained ~7% of body weight by week 68, while those who continued lost an additional 7.9%.
- **SURMOUNT-4:** tirzepatide withdrawal led to ~14% weight regain vs continued 5% additional loss over ~1 year.
- **Why:** GLP-1 drugs don't "reset" your biology. Appetite signaling returns to baseline when the drug leaves your system.
- **Obesity is a chronic disease.** The FDA labels for Wegovy and Zepbound describe them as "chronic" medications — meant to be taken indefinitely.
- **Lifestyle alone can preserve some (but usually not all) lost weight** if started before stopping the drug.

## What the Trial Data Shows

### STEP 1 Extension (Wilding et al., 2022)

The original STEP 1 trial showed semaglutide produced ~14.9% weight loss over 68 weeks. A subset of patients was then followed for an additional 52 weeks after stopping both the drug and the lifestyle program. Published in [Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism](https://dom-pubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/dom.14725), the results were stark:

- Mean weight regain: **two-thirds of the weight lost during treatment**
- At one year off-drug, patients had retained only about 5.6% of body weight loss
- Cardiometabolic improvements (A1C, lipids, blood pressure) also largely reverted

### STEP 4 (Rubino et al., 2021)

STEP 4 was designed specifically to test what happens when you stop. All patients received 20 weeks of semaglutide titration. Then they were randomized to either continue semaglutide or switch to placebo for 48 more weeks.

- **Continued semaglutide group:** lost an additional 7.9% body weight
- **Switched to placebo group:** regained 6.9% body weight
- **Net difference at week 68:** ~15 percentage points

The trial was published in [JAMA](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/2777886) and essentially established that semaglutide must be continued for weight-loss benefits to persist.

### SURMOUNT-4 (Tirzepatide, 2024)

SURMOUNT-4 did the same thing for tirzepatide. Patients took 36 weeks of open-label tirzepatide, losing an average of 20.9% body weight. Then they were randomized to continued tirzepatide or placebo for 52 weeks.

- **Continued tirzepatide:** lost an additional 5.5% body weight
- **Switched to placebo:** regained 14.0% body weight

The [JAMA publication](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/2813962) confirmed the same pattern: withdrawal produces substantial regain, continued treatment maintains and modestly extends loss.

## Why This Happens

The biology of weight regain after GLP-1 withdrawal comes down to three overlapping explanations.

### 1. The Drug Is Doing the Work

GLP-1 drugs work by continuously activating GLP-1 receptors in the hypothalamus and slowing gastric emptying. When the drug is cleared from your system (roughly 5–7 weeks after the last dose for semaglutide), the receptors go back to their natural, intermittent activation pattern. Your body's hunger signaling returns to baseline.

There's no "reset" happening. The drug isn't retraining your neurons or permanently altering your metabolism. It's providing a steady satiety signal that disappears when dosing stops.

### 2. Set Point Theory

Your body has a defended "set point" weight — a weight it actively tries to maintain via hormonal, neural, and metabolic adjustments. When you lose weight (by any method), the body responds by increasing hunger hormones (ghrelin), decreasing satiety hormones (leptin), and lowering resting metabolic rate. This is true for every weight-loss method, not just GLP-1 drugs.

What GLP-1s do is *override* the set-point defense while they're active. Remove the override and the defense snaps back into place. See the [NIH overview of weight regulation](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK279053/) for the full biology.

### 3. Learned Eating Behaviors Fade Fast

Many patients develop healthier eating patterns on semaglutide (smaller portions, higher protein, less alcohol) that they attribute to "willpower" but are actually supported by the drug's appetite effects. When appetite returns, those patterns become hard to maintain without the pharmacological assist.

## Who Regains the Most?

Not everyone regains equally. Factors associated with **more** regain after stopping:

- Longer time on the drug (paradoxically — more weight lost = more to potentially regain)
- Higher starting BMI
- No continued lifestyle program
- Abrupt discontinuation instead of taper
- Stopping at maintenance dose vs. having tapered first

Factors associated with **less** regain:

- Strong resistance-training routine maintained through and after stopping
- Structured high-protein diet maintained
- Support from a registered dietitian
- Slower, deliberate taper of the drug

## Should You Plan to Stop?

In 2026, the medical consensus — reflected in the FDA labels for both Wegovy and Zepbound — is that these drugs are **chronic treatments for a chronic disease**. The FDA Wegovy label specifically states that it is indicated for "chronic weight management" and makes no suggestion that patients should plan for a finite course.

That said, there are valid reasons to stop:

- **Cost** becomes unsustainable
- **Side effects** become unmanageable
- **Pregnancy** (GLP-1s should be stopped at least 2 months before conception per FDA labels)
- **Medical contraindications** develop
- **Goals achieved and patient prefers not to continue**

If any of these apply, the question becomes how to minimize regain — not whether it will happen.

## Strategies to Minimize Regain

### 1. Taper Rather Than Stop Abruptly

Step down through dose levels over 2–4 months instead of stopping at maintenance dose. This gives your body a gentler transition and gives you time to establish lifestyle patterns.

Typical taper for semaglutide from 2.4 mg:
- 2.4 mg → 1.7 mg for 4 weeks
- 1.7 mg → 1.0 mg for 4 weeks
- 1.0 mg → 0.5 mg for 4 weeks
- 0.5 mg → stop

### 2. Lock In Lifestyle Habits Before Stopping

The habits you have on the last day of semaglutide are the habits you'll rely on afterward. For at least 3 months before tapering:

- Track protein intake (~1.2 g/kg body weight)
- Resistance train 3x/week
- Weigh yourself weekly at minimum
- Build a sustainable meal template (see [what to eat on Ozempic](/learn/glp-1-medications/what-to-eat-on-ozempic))

### 3. Consider a Lower Maintenance Dose Instead

For many patients, a lower "maintenance" dose (0.5 mg or 1.0 mg semaglutide, or 5 mg tirzepatide) can preserve most of the weight loss at lower cost and with fewer side effects than the full maintenance dose. This is off-label and requires prescriber collaboration, but it's increasingly common.

### 4. Plan for a Restart

If cost is the issue, consider an alternating strategy: 6–12 months on-drug followed by 3–6 months off with tight lifestyle adherence, then back on. This is speculative — the clinical evidence for cyclical use is thin — but it's better than a one-time stop with no plan.

## Continue vs Stop: A Side-by-Side

| Outcome | Continue | Stop (no plan) | Stop (with structured plan) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weight loss maintained | ~100% | ~33% | ~50–70% (patient-dependent) |
| Monthly cost | $149–$1,349 | $0 | $0 plus nutrition/training |
| Side effects ongoing | Yes | No | No |
| Cardiovascular benefit | Maintained | Reduced | Partially maintained |
| Risk of rebound | Low | High | Moderate |

## What About Withdrawal Symptoms?

Semaglutide and tirzepatide do not produce classical "withdrawal" in the pharmacological sense. There are no receptor-dependence syndromes, no rebound anxiety, no physical dependence. What patients experience as "withdrawal" is almost always:

1. Returning appetite (often dramatic)
2. Returning food cravings
3. Returning capacity to eat larger meals
4. Emotional frustration as the "effortless" weight management ends

These are the expected consequences of stopping, not a withdrawal syndrome. They typically begin within 2–4 weeks of the last dose and peak by weeks 6–10 as blood levels fully clear.

## Pros and Cons of Stopping

**Pros:**

- No more side effects
- Significant cost savings
- Freedom from weekly injections
- Return to normal appetite (if you missed it)

**Cons:**

- Most patients regain the majority of lost weight
- Cardiometabolic gains reverse
- "Food noise" returns
- Restarting after a long gap usually means re-titrating from the lowest dose

## Bottom Line

The science on stopping semaglutide is clear and consistent: most patients regain most of the weight within a year. This isn't anyone's fault — it's how the drug works and how obesity biology works. If you must stop (cost, side effects, pregnancy), you can minimize regain with a taper, locked-in lifestyle habits, and a realistic expectation that some regain is likely.

For most patients who are tolerating the drug well, the best evidence-based answer in 2026 is: **don't stop unless you have to**. GLP-1 drugs are chronic treatments for a chronic disease, the same way blood-pressure medications are chronic treatments for hypertension. If cost is your reason for considering stopping, explore compounded options via our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) or run the numbers in our [savings calculator](/tools/glp1-savings-calculator) before making a decision you'll have to reverse.

title: Manufacturer Coupons for Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Zepbound
excerpt: A current guide to the Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly savings card programs — who qualifies, how much you save, and the fine print most patients miss.
author: Weight Loss Rankings Editorial Team

sources.label: Wegovy Savings Offer (official). Novo Nordisk.
Ozempic Savings Offer (official). Novo Nordisk.
Zepbound Savings Card (official). Eli Lilly.
Mounjaro Savings Card (official). Eli Lilly.
Pharmaceutical Industry Competition Enforcement. U.S. Federal Trade Commission.

body:
Manufacturer savings cards are the single most underused lever in GLP-1 drug pricing. Patients fixate on insurance and compounded pharmacies, but Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly both run generous savings programs that can cut costs by 50–90% for eligible patients. The catch: eligibility is narrower than the ads suggest, the benefits have caps and expiration dates, and the programs change quarterly.

This guide is the current (as of April 2026) state of manufacturer coupons for Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Zepbound. Every number here should be verified directly on the manufacturer's website before you use it — programs change without notice.

**Last verified:** April 6, 2026. Always confirm current terms at the official program sites linked below.

## Key Takeaways

- **Novo Nordisk Wegovy Savings Card:** as low as $0/month (commercially insured with coverage) or $499/month (commercially insured without coverage).
- **Novo Nordisk Ozempic Savings Card:** as low as $25/month for commercially insured patients.
- **Eli Lilly Zepbound Savings Card:** as low as $25/month with commercial insurance coverage; up to $650 off without coverage.
- **Eli Lilly Mounjaro Savings Card:** as low as $25/month with commercial insurance coverage.
- **Government insurance is excluded:** Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, and VA are not eligible for any of these cards.
- **Benefits are capped** both monthly (maximum savings per fill) and in total (maximum fills per year).

## How Manufacturer Savings Cards Work

A savings card (sometimes called a copay card or patient assistance coupon) is a coupon the pharmaceutical manufacturer pays to the pharmacy on your behalf. You present the card alongside your insurance at the pharmacy counter, and the pharmacy processes it as a secondary payer. The manufacturer reimburses the pharmacy for the discount.

The main purpose: lower your out-of-pocket cost while keeping list prices high. It's a win for patients (lower cost), a win for manufacturers (more patients on-drug), and a loss for insurers (who lose some negotiating leverage). This is why federal programs (Medicare, Medicaid) are prohibited by anti-kickback statutes from accepting these cards.

For the full pricing context across all pathways, see [the real cost of Wegovy](/learn/cost-and-insurance/wegovy-cost-real).

## Novo Nordisk Wegovy Savings Card

Wegovy's savings program is the most important because Wegovy has the highest list price ($1,349/month) and the lowest insurance coverage rate.

### If your insurance covers Wegovy

- **Monthly cost:** as low as $0
- **Maximum savings per month:** typically capped at $225
- **Duration:** up to 13 fills
- **Requires:** commercial insurance that covers Wegovy; enrollment through the Wegovy savings program

### If your insurance does NOT cover Wegovy (or excludes obesity coverage)

- **Monthly cost:** $499 flat
- **Duration:** up to 13 fills
- **Requires:** commercial insurance enrollment (any plan, even if Wegovy isn't on formulary)

### Who is eligible

- Adults 18+ with a valid Wegovy prescription
- Have commercial/private insurance (employer-sponsored, marketplace, or individual)
- Not enrolled in Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, VA, Indian Health Services, or similar federal programs
- U.S. residents (including Puerto Rico)

### Who is NOT eligible

- Patients with any government insurance
- Uninsured patients (the card requires active commercial insurance to process)
- Patients in states that prohibit manufacturer coupons (Massachusetts historically had restrictions; check current state law)

Enroll directly at the [official Wegovy savings offer page](https://www.wegovy.com/coverage-and-savings/save-on-wegovy.html).

## Novo Nordisk Ozempic Savings Card

Ozempic's program is narrower because Ozempic is primarily covered by insurance for diabetes patients.

### Terms

- **Monthly cost:** as low as $25
- **Maximum savings per month:** $150
- **Maximum annual savings:** $1,800 (12 fills)
- **Requires:** type 2 diabetes diagnosis, commercial insurance, valid Ozempic prescription

### Who is eligible

- Adults with type 2 diabetes
- Have commercial/private insurance covering Ozempic for diabetes
- Not enrolled in any government insurance

Enroll at the [official Ozempic savings page](https://www.ozempic.com/savings-and-resources/save-on-ozempic.html).

## Eli Lilly Zepbound Savings Card

Lilly's Zepbound savings program is structured similarly to Wegovy's, with some key differences.

### If your insurance covers Zepbound

- **Monthly cost:** as low as $25
- **Maximum savings per fill:** typically $150–$300
- **Duration:** up to 13 fills per year

### If your insurance does NOT cover Zepbound

- **Monthly savings:** up to $650 off list price (~$550–$650 per vial or pen depending on dose)
- **Duration:** up to 7 fills in some variants, varies over time
- **Requires:** commercial insurance (even if not covering Zepbound), valid prescription

### The Zepbound Self-Pay Vial Program (2024+)

Eli Lilly launched a direct-to-patient vial program in 2024 that offers single-use vials of Zepbound at fixed prices to patients paying cash:

- **2.5 mg vial:** $349/month
- **5 mg vial:** $499/month (dropping to $399 in some promotions)
- **7.5 mg vial:** $549/month
- **10 mg vial:** $599/month

This is NOT a coupon — it's a direct purchase program. It's available to patients with or without insurance and is shipped through Lilly's self-pay pharmacy. It's one of the most competitive brand-name pricing options available in 2026. See the [official Zepbound savings page](https://www.zepbound.lilly.com/coverage-savings) for current terms.

## Eli Lilly Mounjaro Savings Card

Mounjaro's program is for patients with type 2 diabetes.

### Terms

- **Monthly cost:** as low as $25 with commercial insurance coverage
- **Monthly savings:** up to $150 per fill without coverage
- **Requires:** type 2 diabetes diagnosis, commercial insurance, valid prescription

Enroll at the [official Mounjaro savings page](https://www.mounjaro.com/savings-resources).

## All Four Programs at a Glance

| Drug | Best case monthly | Without coverage (commercial insurance) | Duration | Government insurance? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wegovy | $0 | $499 | 13 fills | Not eligible |
| Ozempic | $25 | Not available (diabetes only) | 12 fills | Not eligible |
| Zepbound | $25 | $349–$649 (vial program) | 13 fills | Not eligible |
| Mounjaro | $25 | Limited | 12 fills | Not eligible |

## Common Fine Print You Should Know

### 1. "Up to 13 fills" is not a calendar year

The 13-fill limit is consecutive from enrollment, not calendar-year based. If you enroll in March, your 13 fills cover you through roughly the following March.

### 2. Re-enrollment may be required

At the end of 13 fills, you may need to re-enroll in the next year's program, which may have different terms. Don't assume today's card will work next year.

### 3. State law restrictions exist

A handful of states have historically restricted or prohibited manufacturer copay coupons for various drugs (e.g., Massachusetts had limits for branded drugs with generic alternatives). GLP-1s don't have generics, but always verify state rules on the manufacturer page.

### 4. Pharmacy processing varies

Some pharmacies don't process savings cards at the counter and require you to submit receipts for reimbursement. Ask before filling. Mail-order and specialty pharmacies sometimes have different processes than retail.

### 5. You can't combine cards with Medicare Part D

Even if you're eligible for Part D coverage, using a manufacturer card is prohibited by federal law for Medicare beneficiaries. This catches many retirees off guard.

### 6. Cards don't count toward your deductible

Savings from the card typically don't count toward your insurance deductible or out-of-pocket maximum. This matters if you have a high-deductible plan.

## Who Should Use Which Program

**Use the Wegovy savings card if:** you have commercial insurance, want brand-name Wegovy, and either have or don't have Wegovy coverage.

**Use the Ozempic savings card if:** you have type 2 diabetes and commercial insurance covering Ozempic.

**Use the Zepbound savings card / self-pay vial program if:** you want brand-name tirzepatide for weight loss and have commercial insurance.

**Use the Mounjaro savings card if:** you have type 2 diabetes and commercial insurance covering Mounjaro.

**Don't bother with manufacturer cards if:**

- You have Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, or VA coverage (you're excluded)
- You're uninsured (commercial insurance is required)
- Your monthly cost on compounded semaglutide or tirzepatide is already below the savings card price

## Compounded Alternatives

For patients who don't qualify for savings cards — or who qualify but still find $499/month too expensive — compounded semaglutide and tirzepatide remain the most competitive pricing:

- **Compounded semaglutide:** $129–$299/month
- **Compounded tirzepatide:** $199–$399/month

See our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) and [compounded semaglutide safety guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety) before choosing this path.

## Do's and Don'ts

**Do:**

- Verify current program terms on the manufacturer site before enrolling
- Bring your card (or app screenshot) to the pharmacy at every fill
- Ask the pharmacist to confirm the discount was applied before paying
- Re-enroll annually if you plan to continue
- Track your fill count (13 is a limit that sneaks up)

**Don't:**

- Assume savings cards work with any insurance
- Combine cards with government insurance — it's federal fraud
- Skip reading the fine print on maximum benefit caps
- Rely on outdated coupon numbers from unofficial sites — always go to the manufacturer's page

## Bottom Line

Manufacturer savings cards are the best pricing path for commercially insured patients who want brand-name GLP-1 drugs in 2026. The Wegovy card in particular is underused — it brings a $1,349/month drug down to $499/month (or less) for patients whose insurance doesn't cover it. The Zepbound self-pay vial program is competitive with compounded pricing for some doses.

Before making any pricing decision, run your specific situation through our [savings calculator](/tools/glp1-savings-calculator) and compare against compounded options in our [best providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers). And always — *always* — confirm the current savings card terms on the manufacturer site before filling your prescription. Terms change quarterly.

title: Ozempic and Gallbladder Issues: What the Research Actually Shows
excerpt: A clear-eyed look at the elevated risk of gallbladder disease on GLP-1 drugs — what the JAMA meta-analysis found, why it happens, and who's at higher risk.
author: Weight Loss Rankings Editorial Team

sources.label: He L, Wang J, Ping F, et al. Association of Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonist Use With Risk of Gallbladder and Biliary Diseases: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Intern Med. 2022;182(5):513-519. PMID: 35404393.
Wilding JPH, Batterham RL, Calanna S, et al. Once-Weekly Semaglutide in Adults with Overweight or Obesity (STEP 1). N Engl J Med. 2021;384(11):989-1002. PMID: 33567185.
FDA Wegovy Prescribing Information — Warnings and Precautions. U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
